tweets.text:
Securitize went public via a SPAC and immediately tokenized its stock to trade onchain.

I wrote on my blog that I wouldn't farm it, despite Securitize being the No. 1 RWA platform with $4B in distributed value vs Ondo's $3.7B in 2nd place.

(though $BUIDL alone is $2.2B, and my main interest is tokenized stocks, where Securitize ranks 3rd)

No token means nothing to farm, but tokenizing the equity right after the IPO makes it a more interesting play. $GLXY went a similar way.

No token also means clear ownership and none of the token<>equity dilemma that $VVV suffers from now.

But a token is an amazing fundraising tool and, what matters to me, a way to reward loyal users.

$VVV had a juicy airdrop for early users (thanks for that), but post TGE the token is a nuisance: e-begooors keep complaining and the team burns energy on them.

What we're seeing now is another path:

- Backpack airdropped $BP but gives equity access to anyone staking the token for a year

- Kast invited its most active users to receive equity instead of points

So there's a way to reward users with equity. Securitize just didn't do it.

After years of zero tokenomics innovation, new models are emerging that balance the token (fundraising, rewards, utility) with equity (ownership).

Not an easy balance to strike.

I wouldn't be surprised if investors end up preferring one asset only.

Ownership tokens, MetaDAO style. Or tokenized equity.

I still like tokens though.

The airdrop is still the purest early-user reward, even when the real value sits in the equity.

BREAKING: Since the start of 2025, US jobs numbers have now been revised down in 14 out of 17 months by a total of -710,000 jobs.

May and April jobs numbers were revised down by a total of -74,000, the largest 2-month downward revision since December. 

April jobs were revised down by -31,000, to +148,000, while May jobs were revised down by -43,000, to +129,000.

This means -41,765 jobs have been revised out of previously reported data, on average, in each month of this period.

If we apply this average to the +57,000 June nonfarm payrolls, it would imply just ~15,000 jobs were added last month.

Job market revisions are concerning.

tweets.text:
Foreign investors are piling into US equities at a record pace:

Cumulative year-to-date inflows from global investment funds into US equities are up to ~2.5% of their total assets under management.

This percentage has more than DOUBLED since May alone.

This is also significantly above the 2002-2025 average of -0.3% in outflows for this point in the year.

By comparison, excluding the top 10% with the largest inflows and the bottom 10% with the largest outflows of the 2002-2025 period, global fund inflows averaged just ~1.5% by this point in the year.

The YTD pace has already exceeded the full-year total recorded in the middle 50% of years since 2002.

Current demand for US equities is unprecedented.

What is happening with the US labor market?

The difference between nonfarm payrolls and the household survey was a massive 564,000 jobs in June.

This comes as Thursday’s job report showed +57,000 non-farm payrolls were added last month.

At the same time, -507,000 Americans lost their job, the 3rd-largest drop since January 2024, according to the household survey.

The household survey is a closely followed metric because it counts each worker only once, even if they hold multiple jobs.

Year-to-date, total employment in the household survey has declined -1.7 million, to 162.26 million, the lowest since December 2024.

Over the same period, total nonfarm employment has risen +552,000, to a record 158.98 million.

Something does not add up here.

This is a naive take.

So you’re telling me that tokenizing equity and putting onchain is an inferior record keeping system to existing archaic ownership records on mutable centralized servers? 

We talk about the RWA boom and run a mile when we hear the term “security”. But why is a tokenized equity instrument as a security a bad thing?

Yes there are more compliance requirements but, reality check, this is what’s going to need to happen for businesses to come onchain, and especially for token holder rights to be construed as legally on a par with other senior stakeholder (like equity) ownership rights 

Regulators will eventually catch up. Globally aligned standards will be accelerated and the old guard will be decommissioned in the next decade 

You’ll look back on this grey area with 2020 vision and kick yourself that you didn’t see this 

Yes, there will be nuance, no one size will fit all, and aligning interests / enabling protection for “tokenhokders” (which WILL become equity holders in time for many entities) will be imperative

But I guarantee you that blockchain will be the technology that underpins capital markets in the not too distant future 

The future is onchain, and it’ll be a mix of tokens and equities

It baffles me that most people don’t have this foresight

I’m talking generally about the underlying tech 

Dual models will work so long as the token holder rights are clearly defined and people know what they’re buying when they invest in the token

Venice is clear that the token holds value now because equity + token incentives re aligned. That’ll shift over time if investors sell their token so it’s worth monitoring

There are a myriad of other ways to set up dual systems

@MeshClans 🎯

@Big_Ohms_ It’s a necessity and a prerequisite for adoption

@Exchequerfi Whatever rights are attributed from the outset - it’ll be dependant on what’s in the SAFT / other contracts

It’ll all depend on being enforceable in court 

I agree that if a company attributed zero clarity around tokenized asset ownership then it’s worthless 

But to say a dual token/ equity model will never work is wrong. It’ll be dependant on the contracts underpinning the relationship between the token and underlying asset - whether that’s equity or otherwise

@kantianum For as long as the equity holders, hold the token and demand continues for compute via the DIEM token, VVV is ok

If you see indications of that changing then might need to adjust

Leverage in South Korean stocks is out of control:

Assets under management (AUM) in South Korea's leveraged ETFs are up to a record ~$45 billion.

AUM has surged +800% since the start of 2026.

As a result, leveraged exposure as a % of free float market capitalization, the portion of shares actually available for public trading, is up to a record ~2.9%, more than TRIPLING since January.

Meanwhile, the Hong Kong-listed 2x leveraged long SK Hynix ETF rose to ~$15 billion in assets at its peak, the largest single-stock leveraged product in the world.

By comparison, four leading 2x leveraged long ETFs tracking Micron, $MU, Nvidia, $NVDA, Sandisk, $SNDK, and Tesla, $TSLA, have each never exceeded $10 billion in assets.

Leverage in Korea is at extreme levels.

tweets.text:
I think I just found a very interesting stablecoin design.

@Realfi_co is running a 2-token structure on Cardano, with a native EVM launch scheduled shortly after, to separate the peg from the risk.

In most RWA stablecoins, the token itself carries the variability.

Here, there's a separate layer that absorbs that movement before it reaches the peg.

Basically, sUSDr holds the reserve position and takes the hit first, so USDr stays stable.

Structurally, it behaves more like a buffer than a typical stablecoin design.

They're kicking off Phase 1 of the testnet on July 6. I think much more will be coming from them.

Check it out here: https://t.co/fJPiVR2vsU

I'll expand on this in an upcoming post.

I don't think ETH is a shit coin. But I just struggle with viewing it purely in SOV terms, which is what seems to be required to feel bullish. 

The token is struggling to accrue value from what is built on the network, and there are many use cases that could have done well on Ethereum that are doing better on other chains (tokenized stocks on Solana, Perps on Hyperliquid).

I am a fan of Lighter, which utilizes Ethereum's properties well, but again, not sure ETH benefits as much as it should. 

I'll always remain open to changing my mind, but I feel like BTC (SOV) + SOL/HYPE bar bell makes more sense to me.

Just some public market read through from Chinese private VC markets:

Institutions are pouring funds into physical AI and world models. 

1. Large models / LLMs: ~$23.56B
2. AI infrastructure + technical layer: ~$15.74B
3. Embodied intelligence / physical AI: ~$13.36B
4. AIGC applications: ~$8.79B
5. Autonomous driving + other Top-20 cluster: ~$3.82B, but not apples-to-apples with the above.

Some notes: 
- "Early-stage pure foundation-model funding is basically closed".  
Looks like more funding is just being put into existing leaders and going into World Model companies. My guess is that we'll likely see the same in the US with Anthorpic/OpenAI consolidation. 

- "World models have become the biggest consensus in early-stage investment."

Regardless, just an update on developments.

tweets.text: On this day in 1884, Charles Dow published his first stock market average in the Customer's Afternoon Letter, a two-page financial bulletin he co-founded with Edward Jones and Charles Bergstresser. The index tracked 11 companies (nine of them railroads) and closed at 69.93. Dow's idea was simple but radical: track the aggregate movement of stock prices to gauge the health of the broader economy. The concept became the foundation of technical analysis, and the Customer's Afternoon Letter grew into the Wall Street Journal, first published in 1889. The original railroad average evolved into the Dow Jones Transportation Average, while its more famous sibling, the Dow Jones Industrial Average, launched in 1896 and now trades above 40,000.

tweets.text:
📊 Everyone talks about AI as a historic productivity shock, but this is not really visible yet everywhere ⚠

🌏 When we look at productivity per hour worked since 2010, emerging markets show much stronger growth than developed economies. A large part of it is simply economic catch-up with more investment, better infrastructure, more capital per worker, industrial upgrading.

🇺🇸 To me, the most important chart is total factor productivity as we measure the real efficiency of an economy. The US is almost the only major developed economy with a clearly positive trend while many European economies look weak, sometimes even negative. Europe’s problem is not just low growth but also a difficulty in converting innovation, human capital and technology into real efficiency gains.

🇨🇳 The AI indices then tell that the US dominates by a wide margin while China is the only real systemic challenger even though it remains constrained by access to the most advanced chips and by US restrictions.

➡ AI is moving extremely fast but its macro impact will take time. Companies need to reorganise processes, train employees, integrate tools into workflows and actually automate tasks. In that race, the US starts already with a massive lead.

https://t.co/xHgvxwr3wx

tweets.text:
I don't know, but "there is no beef" sounds a lot different to me.

I just saw this and my first instinct was to ignore it. But I think it's important to address because situations like this are exactly how misinformation and false narratives start to spread, and I don't want that to happen.

First, I have nothing but respect for @hosseeb and @tarunchitra. They are genuinely among the few people in this industry who care about pushing it forward, and I've always looked up to their work. Which is exactly why it's disappointing to hear them dunk on someone they've never spoken to and don't actually know anything about.

Now to the substance of what was said:

Tarun made it sound like I promoted some massive staking ponzi or pump and dump scheme to enrich myself. This is simply not true.

Yes, I was very vocal about DeSci. And there is a specific reason for that. Before pivoting my career to crypto, I spent many years in academic cancer research. I know this industry and its pain points inside out. And trust me, there are many. Obviously crypto won't solve all of them, but the nature of tokenization and decentralized capital formation are genuinely well suited to address some of them.

That's why I was excited about it conceptually, and still am. It's also why we made several investments in this space that I talked about publicly. We have sold almost none of these positions, so yes, we are down bad on DeSci. That's part of the venture game and that's totally fine. We always had a long term view on these because, believe it or not, science doesn't get disrupted overnight.

I think it's important to emphasize this because it's probably the assumption behind Tarun's comments. And honestly, it would be a fair concern if it were true. But it isn't.

But maybe, Tarun is right. Maybe I shouldn't have been so loud about it. So if anyone lost money on this, just as we did, I feel genuinely sorry about that. But people who follow me know that this is just my style. I treat my X account as a place to think out loudly and give real insights into my thinking and the Moonrock investment theses. Sometimes that means being very vocal about something early that doesn't work out. But it also means being very vocal about something like Collector when everyone was laughing at it and now it's the entire industry's darling. The toxic nature of CT is that obviously nobody ever focuses on the positive side of things when they can also publicly crucify you instead.

But coming back to DeSci, yes it turned into a massively overhyped, unsustainable pump cycle. Opportunistic people flooded in trying to extract value, and suddenly everyone was a scientist and biotech investor. Classic crypto. So I completely understand why people from the outside might look at it and think it's a scam vertical.

But would we call DePIN a scam vertical because it has broken tokenomics and many projects turned out to be rugs? Obviously not.

The same applies to DeSci. It remains a fascinating vertical with real potential to solve problems I personally experienced and was deeply frustrated by as a researcher. But it's also a vertical that still has many of its own problems to solve and needs time to mature.

I think the biggest issue is just the expectation most crypto investors have. Everyone has been conditioned by memecoin mania and wants to get rich overnight, but that's not how science works. Research has incredibly long feedback cycles. Real breakthroughs take years, sometimes decades. So investors lose interest and move on to the next hot thing.

But that doesn't make it a scam vertical. And it doesn't mean we should put the few brilliant scientists who are genuinely trying to do good for humanity into the same bucket as memecoin pump and dumpers. There's a lot of room between "this cured cancer overnight" and "this was all a scam." The truth, as usual, is somewhere in the middle.

I'd welcome an actual conversation with either of you anytime. My DMs have always been open.

tweets.full_text:
Someone asked if the US economy could be damaged by the high valuations startups get now, and I realized there's a natural protection against this. All the growth is concentrated in the big hits, and they are exactly the companies most able to grow into an "excessive" valuation.

It's certainly true that a startup can be damaged by raising too much and/or at too high a valuation. Raising too much makes you spend too much. And a high valuation increases the chance of a catastrophic down round later on.

So high valuations can be a problem, definitely. But the startups that matter most for economic growth (and investors' returns) are exactly the ones that high valuations are least likely to damage.

When you look back over the last 250 years, the United States has been an amazing place that has given people equal opportunity. People came from all over to work and live well together, becoming prosperous because you could have a good balance of freedom, rule of law, and civility. People were willing to compromise and work together because they recognized that, while imperfect, the greater system was ultimately fair.

Our founding fathers had an aspirational dream of what could be possible, and that dream became a reality. It is one hell of an accomplishment, and we must not lose sight of the principles behind that success.

Which principles do you think have contributed most to America’s success?

#4thOfJuly #America250 #AmericanDream #Principles

This is the most important chart in all of macro and most people still don't get it.

Labor force participation rate vs government debt as a % of GDP, inverted. They move in perfect lockstep.

Government debt has been accumulated to offset the declining working-age population, and from 2008 onwards, that debt has been mostly just servicing old debts while interest payments keep compounding.

Demographics are destiny and ours are already written. The workers for the next decade have already been born. Or rather, they haven't been.

Which means the borrowing and the debasement aren't going to ease. They're going to accelerate.

great take.

the winners in ai will come down to where you choose to spend most of your time (virtual “home”).. viewing the race through this lens hints at who has an advantage today: 

anthropic, openai are both building a home from scratch (cold start) BUT they have the best models so they’re sucking everyone in (data)

meta, apple, google on the other hand cover an enormous surface area for eyeballs already. all they need is a good enough model to keep users within their ecosystem. 

that’s effectively the race we’re watching play out today. round 1 (chatbot) and 2 (enterprise) has been won by openai and anthropic 

incumbents need to make a move now.

your ai agent gets much smarter (and works harder) the more compute you give it

so you likely don’t even need a frontier model to complete most complex tasks - just higher token budgets

explains why a lot of labs are moving to pay-per-usage pricing https://t.co/K41lsRVCK2
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: We also found that the longer a task takes a skilled human, the more compute an agent needs. So, a fixed budget runs out on the longest tasks first, and a failure may mean the run was under-budgeted, not that the agent lacked the capability. https://t.co/Kndo9athj0
